mini_title: Dropbox YC Application on Hacker News
brief_summary: Dropbox's YC application was posted on Hacker News, showcasing its cloud storage service with a screencast. Initial feedback highlighted demo issues and raised concerns about trust, security, and the 'USB drive' tagline. The competitive landscape, including rumored GDrive and existing solutions, was heavily debated, as were scalability and business model viability given Amazon S3 costs.

detailed_summary.heading: Initial Presentation & Core Features
bullets: Dropbox YC application posted on Hacker News with tagline 'Throw away your USB drive' and a screencast demo.

Key Features Clarified by Founder: Works offline by syncing a local folder in the background, providing local I/O speeds.
Designed to work without administrative rights.
Web interface allows for uploads and downloads.
Planned Development: Mac port confirmed to be coming.
Linux port considered 'doable' and potentially useful for syncing files to web hosts.

detailed_summary.heading: Community Feedback & Criticisms
bullets: Demo criticized for being too fast, using folders with too many files, and having poor audio; suggested a slower pace focusing on simple use cases.

Trust & Security Concerns: Users questioned why they should store valuable data on 'Drew's server'.
Founder clarified data is stored on Amazon S3 and encrypted before storage.
Option for client-side encryption using a user-provided passphrase was mentioned.
One user noted client-side encryption still requires faith in the closed-source client.
Tagline Criticism: 'Throw away your USB drive' tagline criticized because Dropbox requires an internet connection.
Also criticized for not being installable on locked-down corporate laptops.

detailed_summary.heading: Competitive Landscape
bullets: Users compared Dropbox to Google's rumored 'GDrive'.
Compared to existing online storage providers like XDrive (offering 5GB free).
Compared to the academic project Coda.
Compared to self-built solutions for Linux users involving FTP, curlftpfs, FUSE, sshfs, and SVN.

Perceived Real Competition: Some argued the real competition was people emailing files to themselves via Gmail or Hotmail.

detailed_summary.heading: Business & Technical Considerations
bullets: Questions about scalability and business model were prominent.
Founder stated they use Amazon S3 for storage.

Profitability Concerns: Commenters pointed out S3's pricing ($0.15/GB-Month for storage, $0.20/GB for transfer) would require Dropbox to charge a premium.
S3 pricing raised questions about profitability.
Trademark Warning: A user warned about a potential trademark issue with the name 'Dropbox'.
Technical Details: The application was written in Python for portability.

detailed_summary.heading: Positive Reception & Founder Insights
bullets: Positive feedback praised the product as 'genius' and 'Something People Want'.
Highlighted the clean user experience and the utility of features like the right-click 'copy public link' option.

Founder's Motivation: Founder shared that the idea evolved from exploring 6-7 concepts.
Idea came from 'scratching his own itch'.
Founder Status: Clarified that while he prototyped Dropbox alone, he was not a single founder.
Confirmed he was applying for the current YC round.

metadata.author: dhouston
metadata.confidence_reason: Algolia item API fetched; linked article fetched
